votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the vowel check, string filtering, and specification logic defined in the Coq code.

The Python code correctly implements the vowel check, string filtering, and specification logic defined in the Coq code.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. is_vowel checks for the same 10 vowel characters (a,e,i,o,u in both cases). filter_string keeps characters where the predicate is true, and problem_51_spec checks that output equals the input with vowels removed (using negb/not is_vowel). The precondition is trivially True in both. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. is_vowel checks the same 10 vowel characters (a,e,i,o,u in both cases). filter_string keeps characters where the predicate is true. problem_51_spec checks if output equals the input string with vowels removed (using negb/not is_vowel). The precondition is trivially True in both. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. is_vowel checks the same 10 vowel characters (a,e,i,o,u in both cases). filter_string keeps characters where the predicate is true. problem_51_spec checks if output equals the input string with vowels removed (using negb/not is_vowel). The precondition is trivially True in both. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.
Both define vowels as AEIOU/a eiou and return the input string with those removed, preserving all other characters and order; precondition is unconstrained.

Both define vowels as aeiou/AEIOU and specify output as input filtered to keep non-vowels; Python’s filter and spec mirror Coq’s filter_string with negated is_vowel. Precondition is True in both. Behavior matches for all ASCII strings.
Both keep exactly the non-vowel characters in order; is_vowel and filtering logic match Coq’s definitions for all ASCII inputs, including newlines.
